text: The surgeon demonstrates trocar placement for a robotic sleeve gastrectomy with hiatal hernia repair, positioning a 12mm trocar, three 8mm trocars, and a 5mm incision for a Nathanson retractor approximately 15 centimeters below the rib edge to elevate the liver.

The surgeon demonstrates trocar placement for a robotic sleeve gastrectomy with hiatal hernia repair, inserting one 12mm trocar, three 8mm trocars, and a 5mm incision for a Nathanson retractor approximately 15 centimeters below the rib edge to elevate the liver and provide operating space.
The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to isolate, cut, and separate the blood vessels from the greater curvature of the stomach, minimizing bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to isolate and cut the blood vessels supplying the stomach along the greater curvature, minimizing bleeding during the separation process.

The surgeon identifies a previously undetected four-centimeter hiatal hernia during the procedure, necessitating a repair to prevent potential reflux.

The surgeon carefully performs blunt dissection to separate the esophagus from adhesions at the diaphragmatic crura, with the inferior vena cava visible beneath the liver on the left side of the screen.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection to separate the esophagus from adhesions at the diaphragmatic cruce. A 36 French Visigi bougie is placed in the esophagus, providing suction to clear the intragastric space and calibrate the staple line for the sleeve gastrectomy.

A 36 French visigi bougie is placed in the patient's stomach, providing calibration for the stapling during the sleeve gastrectomy. The bougie also applies a slight vacuum to the intragastric space, ensuring clear visualization of the staple line and esophagus manipulation.

The surgeon identifies the right and left crura and begins repairing the diaphragmatic hernia, carefully preserving the vagal nerves. A robotic tool is used to gently approximate the tissue using a number one Ethabon suture.

The surgeon uses robotic assistance to re-approximate the crural fibers, employing slip knots to ensure a secure closure. The robotic tools provide enhanced visual control, allowing precise manipulation of the tissue during the diaphragmatic hernia repair.

The surgeon uses robotic assistance to re-approximate the crural fibers of the diaphragm, carefully placing sutures approximately seven to eight millimeters apart to avoid excessive tension and prevent tearing. Slip knots are utilized to secure the repair, with visual cues aiding in assessing tissue tightness.

The surgeon uses three to four sutures to re-approximate the diaphragmatic hernia repair, placing them approximately seven to eight millimeters apart. Care is taken to avoid excessive tension to prevent tearing of the acryl fibers, while being cautious of nearby structures such as the vagus nerve and inferior vena cava.

The surgeon has closed the hiatus, ensuring the repair is not too tight while well approximating the left and right crura. The next step involves initiating the stapling process.

The surgeon inspects the surgical area for any excess fat or herniated omentum that could interfere with the stapling process. The stomach is visually assessed for healthy, viable coloration before initiating the stapling procedure.

The surgeon positions the bougie to calibrate the stapling process, starting approximately four centimeters from the pylorus. A robotic green stapler is used to initiate the staple line, with the SureForm stapling device providing feedback cues to ensure appropriate staple selection based on tissue sensing.

The bougie is positioned to calibrate the stapling process. The surgeon begins the staple line approximately four centimeters from the pylorus using a robotic green stapler. The SureForm stapling device is employed, providing feedback cues to ensure the chosen staple is appropriate for the tissue.

The surgeon aligns the staples along the stomach, ensuring a proper bite without excessive tissue inclusion. The staple line appears hemostatic, and the stapler's angulation facilitates easy manipulation. The stomach is flattened as the surgeon marks up towards the angle of His.

The stomach is flattened to avoid tension, particularly at the angle of His, where leaks are most likely to occur.

The surgeon fires the stapler at the angle of His, ensuring a tension-free staple line. The staple line is carefully placed to avoid leaving excess fundus tissue and to maintain a safe distance from the bougie, ensuring a smooth and healthy tissue alignment.

The surgeon ensures the angle of the staples creates a uniform cylindrical shape of the stomach, avoiding excessive tension and irregularities in the staple line.

An intraoperative endoscopy is performed to inspect the stomach for bleeding and ensure it is a healthy, viable cylindrical tube without torsion. A fibrin hemostatic agent is applied following the inspection.

The surgeon cuts the tip of an NG tube and places it intra-abdominally to use as a suction device. A special fibrin glue is applied to control hemostasis and assist in positioning the stomach to maintain a smooth angle and prevent torsion. A portion of omentum is placed over the stomach. The stomach is then removed through a twelve millimeter incision, which is closed using a Carter Thompson device.
